title: Reproductive Health
subtitle: A visual guide to reproductive well-being, contraception, population control, STIs, infertility, ART, MTP and responsible choices — built for Class 12 Biology and NEET revision.

heroModel.title: Reproductive health shield
heroModel.subtitle: Drag to rotate · scroll to zoom

roadmapTitle: The chapter connects health, prevention, counselling and technology.
roadmapDescription: Think of reproductive health as a complete system: awareness, safe practices, contraception, infection prevention, infertility support and ethical medical care.
flowchart:
flowchart LR
  A[Reproductive health] --> B[Awareness + counselling]
  B --> C[Population control]
  C --> D[Contraceptive methods]
  D --> E[MTP where legal + safe]
  B --> F[STI prevention]
  F --> G[Early diagnosis + treatment]
  B --> H[Infertility support]
  H --> I[ART: IVF, ZIFT, IUT, ICSI, AI]
  I --> J[Ethics + responsible care]

roadmap.title: Meaning of reproductive health
roadmap.summary: Total well-being in reproductive aspects, not merely absence of disease.

roadmap.title: Strategies and awareness
roadmap.summary: Education, counselling, safe practices and access to health services.

roadmap.title: Population explosion
roadmap.summary: Rapid population growth, causes, consequences and birth-control need.

roadmap.title: Contraception
roadmap.summary: Natural, barrier, IUD, oral, injectable, implant and surgical methods.

roadmap.title: Medical termination of pregnancy
roadmap.summary: MTP must be medically safe, legal and ethically guided.

roadmap.title: Sexually transmitted infections
roadmap.summary: Transmission, symptoms, prevention and early treatment.

roadmap.title: Infertility
roadmap.summary: Failure to conceive despite unprotected intercourse can have male or female causes.

roadmap.title: Assisted reproductive technologies
roadmap.summary: IVF, ZIFT, IUT, ICSI, GIFT and artificial insemination help selected infertility cases.

conceptDescription: The chapter is short but very fact-heavy. These cards organise the facts into prevention, control, diagnosis and assisted reproduction.

sections.title: Meaning of reproductive health
sections.description: Reproductive health means physical, emotional, behavioural and social well-being in all reproduction-related aspects.
points: It is broader than absence of reproductive disease.
It includes awareness, responsible behaviour and access to safe health care.
Adolescence education helps prevent myths, unsafe practices and infections.
Mother-child health programmes improve pregnancy and newborn outcomes.
sections.neet: NCERT definition includes total well-being, not only disease-free reproductive organs.

sections.title: Strategies for reproductive health
sections.description: Good reproductive health depends on education, counselling, safe practices and medical support.
points: Sex education should provide accurate information about reproductive organs, puberty and safe practices.
Couples should know birth-control options and infertility care.
Health services include antenatal care, delivery support and postnatal care.
Early detection of reproductive tract problems improves treatment outcomes.
sections.neet: Counselling and awareness are prevention tools, not just social messages.

sections.title: Population explosion and birth control
sections.description: Rapid population growth increases pressure on resources, health services and education.
points: Decline in death rate, maternal mortality and infant mortality can increase population growth.
Better medical care and increased reproductive age population contribute to growth.
Small-family norms and contraception help population stabilisation.
Government programmes encourage responsible family planning.
sections.neet: Population control is linked to contraception, education and delayed marriage.

sections.title: Contraceptive methods
sections.description: Contraceptives prevent pregnancy by blocking gamete meeting, ovulation, implantation or gamete transport.
points: Natural methods include periodic abstinence, withdrawal and lactational amenorrhea.
Barrier methods prevent sperm from reaching ovum and can reduce STI risk.
IUDs increase phagocytosis of sperms or suppress sperm motility/fertilising ability.
Oral pills inhibit ovulation and implantation and alter cervical mucus.
sections.neet: Lactational amenorrhea works only up to about six months after parturition when lactation is intense.

sections.title: Medical termination of pregnancy
sections.description: MTP is intentional termination of pregnancy under medical supervision and legal guidelines.
points: It can be needed for medical, ethical or social reasons under legal rules.
Unsafe abortions are dangerous and must be avoided.
MTP should never be misused for sex-selective practices.
Medical supervision is essential for safety.
sections.neet: MTP is legal only under specific conditions and must be medically safe.

sections.title: Sexually transmitted infections
sections.description: STIs spread mainly through sexual contact and can damage reproductive health if untreated.
points: Examples include gonorrhoea, syphilis, genital herpes, chlamydiasis, hepatitis B and HIV/AIDS.
Symptoms may include discharge, itching, pain, swelling or sores, but some infections may be silent.
Prevention includes safe sex, avoiding multiple partners and using condoms.
Early diagnosis and complete treatment are important.
sections.neet: Hepatitis B and HIV can also spread through blood and from mother to child.

sections.title: Infertility and its causes
sections.description: Infertility is inability to produce children despite unprotected sexual cohabitation, often due to male or female factors.
points: Causes may be physical, congenital, immunological, psychological or disease-related.
Male causes may include low sperm count or poor sperm motility.
Female causes may include ovulation, tubal or uterine problems.
Counselling and diagnosis should precede treatment.
sections.neet: Infertility is a couple-level problem; do not automatically assign it only to the female partner.

sections.title: Assisted reproductive technologies
sections.description: ART helps selected infertile couples by handling gametes or embryos medically.
points: IVF means fertilisation outside the body under simulated conditions.
ZIFT transfers zygote or early embryo up to 8 blastomeres into the fallopian tube.
IUT transfers embryo with more than 8 blastomeres into the uterus.
ICSI injects sperm directly into ovum; artificial insemination places semen into female tract.
sections.neet: ZIFT is up to 8 blastomeres; IUT is more than 8 blastomeres.

modelDescription: The chapter is conceptual, so these original 3D models turn public-health ideas into memorable visual anchors.

models.title: ART laboratory
models.description: IVF, embryo transfer and assisted reproduction.

highYield: Reproductive health means total well-being in reproductive aspects.
Sex education helps remove myths and promotes responsible behaviour.
Contraception helps population control and reproductive planning.
Natural methods include periodic abstinence, withdrawal and lactational amenorrhea.
Lactational amenorrhea is effective only up to about six months with intense lactation.
Barrier methods reduce chances of both pregnancy and STIs.
IUDs can increase phagocytosis of sperms and reduce sperm motility/fertilising capacity.
Oral pills inhibit ovulation and implantation and alter cervical mucus.
Surgical methods are terminal and block gamete transport.
MTP must be safe, legal and medically supervised.
STIs may be symptomatic or silent.
HIV and hepatitis B can spread by sexual contact, blood and mother-to-child transmission.
Infertility may have male or female causes.
ZIFT transfers zygote/early embryo up to 8 blastomeres into fallopian tube.
IUT transfers embryo with more than 8 blastomeres into uterus.

mistakes.title: ZIFT vs IUT
mistakes.body: ZIFT is up to 8 blastomeres into the fallopian tube. IUT is more than 8 blastomeres into the uterus.

mistakes.title: MTP is not casual
mistakes.body: MTP must be legal, medically supervised and ethically used. Unsafe abortions are dangerous.

mistakes.title: Infertility blame trap
mistakes.body: Infertility can be due to either partner or combined factors, so it is treated as a couple-level issue.

quiz.question: Reproductive health means:
quiz.explain: NCERT defines reproductive health as total well-being in all reproductive aspects.

quiz.question: Lactational amenorrhea is generally effective up to:
quiz.explain: It is considered effective for about six months after parturition under intense lactation.

quiz.question: Which method also helps reduce STI transmission?
quiz.explain: Barrier methods like condoms reduce contact and lower STI transmission risk.

quiz.question: Copper-releasing IUDs mainly:
options: Suppress sperm motility and fertilising capacity
quiz.explain: Copper ions suppress sperm motility and fertilising capacity.

quiz.question: Oral contraceptive pills mainly prevent pregnancy by:
quiz.explain: Pills inhibit ovulation and implantation and alter cervical mucus.

quiz.question: Which STI can also spread through blood and mother-to-child transmission?
quiz.explain: HIV and hepatitis B can spread through blood and from infected mother to child.

quiz.question: In ZIFT, embryo stage transferred is usually:
options: Zygote/early embryo up to 8 blastomeres into fallopian tube
quiz.explain: ZIFT transfers zygote or early embryo up to 8 blastomeres into fallopian tube.

quiz.question: IUT means transfer of:
options: Embryo with more than 8 blastomeres into uterus
quiz.explain: IUT transfers embryo with more than 8 blastomeres into the uterus.

quiz.question: ICSI involves:
quiz.explain: In ICSI, sperm is directly injected into the ovum.

quiz.question: Surgical contraceptive methods are called terminal because they:
options: Block gamete transport permanently or long-term
quiz.explain: Vasectomy and tubectomy block gamete transport and are considered terminal methods.

flashcards.back: Total physical, emotional, behavioural and social well-being in reproductive aspects.
Natural contraception due to absence of menstruation during intense lactation, effective about six months postpartum.
Medical termination of pregnancy under legal and medically supervised conditions.
Transfer of zygote or embryo up to 8 blastomeres into fallopian tube.
Transfer of embryo with more than 8 blastomeres into uterus.
Intracytoplasmic sperm injection: direct injection of sperm into ovum.

revisionTable: Inhibit ovulation/implantation and alter cervical mucus
Vasectomy and tubectomy block gamete transport
Safe sex, condoms, early diagnosis, complete treatment
IVF, ZIFT, IUT, ICSI, GIFT and artificial insemination
